What is everyone's preferred software for project management and why do you choose it over others?

My approach is 1) find the common PM tool that your stakeholders are using 2) Understand the team use cases (get feedback) 3) define whether to use that tool or implement a new PM tool that solves the issues while taking into consideration on-ramp up etc. To answer your question - I have used most tools and the one I use the most is Asana. I like the UI and continue to see slight improvements throughout the years. JIRA is the other since all my dev teams use it so it is a lighter lift.
❤️ your thouroughness! Thanks! Agree with your systematic approach.

Start with the business objective. What is the transformation intended to achieve? Often times this is unclear and needs to be defined, which requires a lot of stakeholder discussion. From there you should consider the people, process, and technology changes that would need to occur to enable that transformation. This means doing a lot of legwork on the current state and then aligning stakeholders on the desired future state. A helpful way to do this is through visioning exercises to get people on the same page. From there you can start brainstorming possible solutions - people, process, and technology changes - that would need to occur to achieve this outcome. Then the exercise starts to look more like a product development program, but with extra focus on internal communications to manage and reinforce changes.  Check out the ADKAR model for a helpful framework to think about change management! https://www.prosci.com/resources/articles/why-the-adkar-model-works

embeds.title: The Prosci ADKAR Model: Why it Works
embeds.description: Learn why the Prosci ADKAR Model is effective in guiding individuals through the change process. This individual change model is simple to follow and effective.

messages.content: @Lheia were there any technical/system design question in the online assessment?

No but the role I had an assessment with was a Program Manager role, non-technical. It was all multiple choice/rank. It focused on how I would prioritize tasks or rate solutions (keeping Amazon principles in mind in the background to guide as well). The second half was a personality assessment formated as deciding which of the 2 statements were more true about you, on a scale.

Anyone here have tips or tricks on taking meeting notes? My boss has asked me to take meeting notes more often but I struggle to take notes and being engaged in the meeting. I sometimes record meeting to take notes later but that just means spending more of my time allocated to re-listening to the meeting.

i found microsoft one note to be powerful if you have access to office suit. Notion is good as well.. one note allows outlook integration to prefill attendee names and  allows you to check off names of who really attended and imports agenda (if you have set one) from outlook invite.  Usually provide a context in the meeting invite of what folks are gathering for (problem or topic at hand ) and also if there is any preparatory documents they need to read to be more effective in the meeting (which can be attached or linked to invite and gets carried over to your one note meeting notes with outlook integration). As for the meeting notes format - after the Topic/ Attendees  you can have a summary of what was discussed (100 ft view) and key discussion points/ proposals or opinions shared by participants and Key action items/next steps identified during the meeting and who will be actioning them.  A simple template going into meeting allows you to jot notes in  respective sections easily, I beilieve there is also JIRA integration with OneNote/MS Teams to create tickets from action items so follow ups are easy.  I have also found that allocation of first 2-3  min of a meeting to reiterate the purpose of meeting and also last 2-3 minutes of meeting to summarize the action items/follow up so approriate owners are in agreement helps every one to be on same page. I also found conducting a 25 min or 55 min meeting is more effective some times so last 5 min of that half hour/hour you are giving back to participants and will also allow you to compile your thoughts and reach out to any one from whom you may need a clarification in case you did not get the gist during the meeting. Also people like shorter meetings with strict agenda.
a sample template below
May not be an option. But I have recorded via voice memos app on my iPhone. And import that into word 365. It can generate a transcript for you.

embeds.title: What TPMs Do and What Software Engineers Can Learn From Them
embeds.description: A deep dive with five Technical Program Managers (TPM) on what the role is, how it evolved, and how engineers and managers can benefit from working with TPMs.

messages.content: Looks interesting, will read. I thought there will always be an issue because you might have 5-100+ software engineers per TPM type of ratio.
Yeah, I don't quite agree with that ratio. We generally use 25:1

Highly dependent on your company, but the point is you need a lot more software engineers than TPMs and if you have the same amount of supply with lower demand then this seems to be a big part of the issue.
Also, some companies look down at the role as unnecessary since it’s not the role who is often directly doing the work.
One counter argument I saw is TPMs tend to cap out salary lower than software engineers, but can have greater upward mobility to move into senior leadership roles.
The career piece is not necessarily true, IMO. TPMs have more role clarity and alignment issues than engineers as you go up the chain.

In this context, Senior TPM is usually L5, staff is L6 and Sr Staff is L7.
Thanks - do you know if the staff maps out to an L6 senior at amaxon. Surprised if it maps to a principle since they have so many staff positions

Any comments on this type of situations where the real intent is not to hire you but to learn from you or for the purpose of showing that they did interview people of diversity etc- https://www.quora.com/Do-companies-do-fake-interviews-without-any-actual-intent-of-hiring-the-candidate-maybe-just-to-show-a-diversity-in-the-candidate-interview-pool or https://www.employmentcrossing.com/article/900047459/5-Signs-You-Are-in-a-Fake-Job-Interview/
The tpm leader at Cruise let me know that Amazon L7= Cruise L6.

I was part of mapping the broadband Sr, Staff etc levels to numbers L4....8 etc. I can share more on what to expect. But yes, roughly Amazon L7 maps to L6 but at the end of the day, you have to be able to demonstrate your level in the interview based on the scope you have managed.
Very helpful, thanks
I can tell Zoox does that

Is it uniquely for tpm leveling or overall engineering leveling? My limited data points (SW engineer and TPM) have been amazon L7 = Cruise Principal (assuming 7 or 8?), amazon L6 = Cruise Staff / Cruise Sr. Staff. Haven't seen any down leveled...

For TPMs, I have generally observed Amazon L6 getting mapped to L5 (Senior) and Amazon L7 to L6 (Staff) at both Meta and Cruise. Principal at Cruise is L8 so I doubt that Amazon L7 would map to L8 unless the candidate hits it out of the park during the interview. Then again, I go back to what I said - your interview performance is most important factor. If you are an Amazon L6 and interviewing for L7 role, if you can nail the interview - you will get it.  I have often seen lot of candidates negotiate on level but the interview performance often doesn't justify. That's why always prepare for interviews with the target level in mind. Your answers need to adapt

That's definitely interesting! Thank you for sharing the insights! You surely have more datapoints than me! My personal data point was that I got Cruise L6 offer and Amazon L6 offer. Both are TPM roles. I only have one Amazon L7 to Cruise L8 data point. That's a SW engineer role though. She must've done super well in the interview!

I took PMP about 5 years ago and passed with pretty good scores. I took the self study route and needed more than 6 months. Subject is pretty dry but I had prior project management experience. I was able to relate fairly well. It's a good methodology and you will likely learn lots. Big tech may not care for PMP but there are others that definitely do.

embeds.title: Welcome to Rands Leadership Slack
embeds.description: For a role as crucial as Leadership, there is a shocking lack of required training on the topic. Combined with a tremendous collection of impenetrable literature, leadership is an essential skill learned on the job. The Rands Leadership Slack (“RLS”) exists to help longtime, new, and aspiring leader

Hi anyone see new hire onboarding  in your company recently?   TPM or general tech... Wondering if anyone actually getting offers.

When an engineer says TPM is a useless role what are your assumptions on that engineer?

For example, mine is that there are low level engineers and tired of giving updates to the TPM,  they don’t know what the TPM does after they give the update
It tells me they don't truly understand the role.

I would not judge engineers who think this.  Instead, I would maybe look at the TPM's that are working with the engineer to see they are actually adding value.  It may be that the TPM's aren't adding enough value to the organization.  Then again, it may be a personal matter where an engineer and TPM do not get along.
99% of the case, it is what they read at Blind and they don't have a clue. they just follow the popular hype.

Hi all. I just had my solutions design interview for product technical program manager at meta on Friday . 

It was more of a product sense interview where I give 5 recommendations and pick one or two out of it and talk about how I would take it to the customer but there wasn’t much emphasis on system design or architecture . I had spent all my time preparing the structure and format of a system design with a bit of focus on general assumptions, constraints and success metrics . But the whole interview was 90 percent about how I would design the product and show it to the customer than the how the system will be implementing the requirements . However , during the recruiter call (and a second clarification call) and in the meta final loop resource , it was mentioned that it would be system design oriented . 

Had I known it would be a product sense interview I would have concentrated more on the product management perspective. Is this a miss on my part? Should I mention this misalignment on expectations to the recruiter ? Even though it doesn’t change anything ?

Would appreciate any advice. Thanks

Hi team,
I have a question. Is it safe to take  a role of Program Manager in Amazon. With many layoffs targeted in future. Is it good to go ahead and accept the offer

nowhere is safe. but inside Amazon, it will just depend on the org. Q3 results are strong, it should be fine for AWS and Ads but you never know. with RTO, several people are already leaving lately, they may not need another layoff with the current pace of attrition
Yes hope no layoffs happen next. Thanks for the reply

Unfortunately this has been VERY common in my experience. What I hear from the Recruiter most often is not largely what happens during the interview rounds. 9 out of 10 times it's the case where the focus is different than described. You could say something, but I'm not sure it will matter much.

Is Senior TPM at Google same comparable level to Sr TPM (L6) at Amazon? Or it’s more senior?
I would say that typically Amazon leveling is one above standard leveling at Google and Meta. So for example, an L6 at Amazon would probably more equate to an L5 at Google or Meta.

although this is true for salary/organization-wise, most Amazon L6s have more impact/scale than Meta/Google IC6s. So Amazon is giving less salary for a high impact work. I am  still not sure if it is good or bad.
